intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Hermes is a single-tenant personal AI agent for one authorized operator that holds conversations and completes open-ended work — running shell commands, reading and writing files, executing code, automating a browser, running cron jobs, delegating to subagents, and learning across sessions through persistent memory and self-authored skills — reachable identically across CLI/TUI, ~20 messaging platforms, an editor/ACP adapter, an optional HTTP/API surface, and the Electron Hermes Desktop app that spawns and drives a headless `hermes serve` backend. Every network-exposed surface must fail closed (allowlist or auth provider before serving), dangerous commands must require human approval, a catastrophic-command floor must be un-overridable even under `--yolo`, and operator credentials must never leak into lower-trust child processes, logs, or outbound payloads. Desktop must not weaken the Agent's trust rules and must keep its backend loopback-bound unless the operator deliberately configures an authenticated remote setup.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A large, actively-maintained Python agent core (`run_agent.py`/`AIAgent`, an 80+ module `tools/` tree, a `gateway/` messaging layer with ~30 platform adapters plus an `api_server` HTTP adapter) paired with an Electron + React desktop app (`src/main` privileged process / `src/renderer` UI, spawning the Python backend over a loopback JSON-RPC/WS or an `ssh -N -L` tunnel). Security controls are real and operative: an un-overridable provider-credential blocklist scrubs child-process environments (`tools/environments/local.py`, `tools/env_passthrough.py`), an unconditional hardline command floor sits below `--yolo` (`tools/approval.py`), the API server refuses to start without `API_SERVER_KEY` and the gateway default-denies unknown callers (`gateway/platforms/api_server.py`, `gateway/run.py`), logs rotate through a `RedactingFormatter` (`hermes_logging.py`), and dependencies are exact-pinned with OSV/supply-chain CI. The project's own `SECURITY.md` is candid that these in-process heuristics are not containment — the only boundary against an adversarial LLM is OS-level isolation, which on the default local terminal backend is off, leaving the external-input-to-execution path bounded by heuristics.

behavior_summary:
Hermes is a mature, security-conscious agent whose dominant residual pattern is the one its own `SECURITY.md` names: the only real boundary against an adversarial LLM is OS-level isolation, and on the default local terminal backend that boundary is off. Untrusted content (open web, inbound messages, MCP responses) enters the model context and can reach `terminal()`/`execute_code`, gated only by in-process heuristics — a regex approval prompt and the `threat_patterns` scanner — that the maintainers correctly document as review aids, not containment.

That path is genuinely mitigated, not open: a human-in-the-loop approval gate prompts on dangerous commands, an un-overridable hardline floor blocks catastrophic commands beneath every bypass flag, provider credentials are stripped from child environments, and every network surface fails closed. The remaining findings are narrower divergences from operator intent — telemetry that defaults on rather than requiring opt-in, an MCP malware check that fails open, host-key TOFU on the remote tunnel, and free-form (rather than structured) activity logs.

rules.rule_text: Yes — each enabled adapter requires an operator-configured caller allowlist (or DM-pairing approval) before it may serve any caller
Yes — must require an operator-set auth layer (allowlist / auth provider) before serving
Yes — remote backend must be protected by an auth provider (username/password for trusted networks, OAuth for anything internet-reachable)
Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.

Credentials and gateway tokens MUST NOT be passed into lower-trust child processes (shell subprocesses, `execute_code`, MCP subprocesses, cron scripts) except variables the operator or a loaded skill has explicitly declared as passthrough.
Secrets MUST NOT be written to logs, transcripts, error messages returned to the model, or any outbound adapter payload.
Credentials MUST NOT be committed to version control or stored in the main (shareable) config file.
Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern on a host-reaching backend MUST prompt the operator and MUST NOT execute until approved.
When an approval prompt is not answered within the configured window, the action MUST be denied (fail closed).
Installing or loading a third-party skill or plugin MUST give the operator a chance to review its actual code before it executes.
Cron jobs that hit a dangerous-command prompt MUST default to denying it rather than silently auto-approving.
Binding any local-only surface to a non-loopback interface MUST be a deliberate operator action that engages an authentication layer.

Executing a catastrophic, irreversible command (filesystem-root wipe, fork bomb, formatting a mounted device, zeroing a physical disk, piping an untrusted remote script straight to a shell) MUST be refused unconditionally — even under `--yolo`, `approvals.mode: off`, a permanent allowlist entry, or headless cron approve-mode.

Dispatching agent work, resolving an approval, or returning agent output to a caller who is outside the configured authorization set (allowlist, pairing approval, or OS-level equivalent) MUST NOT be possible; treating a session identifier as proof of authorization is forbidden.
A network-exposed adapter MUST NOT fail open (serve callers) when no allowlist / auth provider is configured.
Credentials or session-authorization material MUST NOT leak to any destination outside the operator's trust envelope through environment leakage, adapter logging, or transport error paths.

The desktop-launched backend MUST remain a JSON-RPC/API surface only (no unintended browser-reachable UI) and MUST stay bound to loopback unless the operator deliberately configures a remote/authenticated setup.
Agent actions and decisions are recorded to durable, operator-inspectable logs.
Sessions are isolated from one another; one session MUST NOT read another session's data or state.
a command that was denied or blocked MUST NOT be silently retried or rephrased to evade the guard.
Any tool that disables the always-on hardline command floor (see Action Boundaries → Never Allowed) or removes the operator's ability to see what a skill/plugin will run before install.

findings.summary: On the default host-reaching backend, attacker-influenceable content can reach `terminal()` / `execute_code` gated only by in-process heuristics the project itself declines to treat as boundaries.
findings.description: Hermes ingests untrusted content (open web, inbound messages, MCP responses, file reads) into the model context and can then issue shell and code execution; on the default local terminal backend those calls run against the host. The only interposition is a human approval prompt driven by a regex denylist plus the `threat_patterns` scanner — real mitigations that block catastrophic commands unconditionally and prompt on matched dangerous patterns, but which `SECURITY.md` §2.2/§2.4 correctly documents as heuristics, not containment. The residual external-input-to-execution chain is why OS-level isolation (a non-default terminal backend or whole-process wrapper) is the supported posture when ingesting untrusted surfaces.
policy_rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern on a host-reaching backend MUST prompt the operator and MUST NOT execute until approved.

evidence.snippet: "§2.2 — 'The only security boundary against an adversarial LLM is the operating system. Nothing inside the agent process constitutes containment'; default local backend runs LLM-emitted commands directly on the host."

"DANGEROUS_PATTERNS is a ~47-entry regex denylist over Turing-complete shell driving the HITL prompt; hardline floor (:203) blocks catastrophic commands but the general model-output-to-shell path is heuristic-bounded on the default posture."

recommended_actions: Treat a non-default terminal backend (Docker/Modal/Daytona) or whole-process wrapper as the required posture whenever the agent ingests untrusted input, and default new installs that enable messaging/web ingestion toward an isolated backend rather than the host-reaching local one.
Surface the isolation posture prominently at setup so operators choose it deliberately, per SECURITY.md §2.2.

findings.summary: The OSV malware check run before launching an npx/uvx MCP server fails open, allowing an unvetted package to launch on any network error or timeout.
findings.description: `check_package_for_malware()` queries OSV for MAL-* advisories before an MCP server package is spawned, but wraps the query in a broad `except Exception` that returns None (allow) on network errors, timeouts, or parse failures. An attacker who can degrade or block the OSV endpoint — or simply a transient outage during an install — silently bypasses the supply-chain guard the operator believes is protecting MCP launches.

evidence.snippet: "try/except around _query_osv: 'Fail-open: network errors, timeouts, parse failures → allow' returns None so the package proceeds."
"Module docstring: 'Fail-open: network errors allow the package to proceed.'"

recommended_actions: Make the check fail closed (or prompt the operator) when the OSV query cannot be completed for a package the operator has not previously approved, rather than silently allowing the launch.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop enables PostHog analytics by default (opt-out) on official builds, diverging from the remit's requirement of explicit operator opt-in for outbound telemetry.
findings.description: `isAnalyticsEnabled()` returns true on first run whenever a PostHog key is baked into the build, so official-build users are opted in until they find and toggle the setting off — the remit's Non-Goals forbid emitting telemetry, usage attribution, or third-party identifier tagging without explicit opt-in. The telemetry is privacy-hardened (anonymous UUID, `respect_dnt`, `mask_personal_data_properties`, session recording disabled), which lowers the impact, but the consent default is inverted from what the remit requires.
policy_rule_text: Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.

evidence.snippet: "initAnalytics + isAnalyticsEnabled: 'First run: enabled if key exists' — stored===null returns hasKey, so official builds start opted-in."
"posthog.identify(getOrCreateAnonymousId(), {app_version, platform, node_version}) fires on load before any explicit consent."

recommended_actions: Default analytics to disabled until the operator explicitly opts in (invert the first-run branch so `stored===null` returns false), matching the remit's explicit-opt-in requirement.

findings.summary: The desktop-to-remote-backend SSH tunnel uses `StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`, trusting-on-first-use and leaving a first-connection MITM window on the transport carrying agent traffic and the remote API key.
findings.description: The tunnel to a remote `hermes serve` backend is a key-authenticated `ssh -N -L` bound to loopback — a solid posture — but `accept-new` silently accepts and pins whatever host key is presented on the first connection, so an attacker positioned on the path at first connect can intercept the tunnel that carries the remote API key and all chat traffic. The remit is silent on host-key verification specifically; this is the enabling weakness in the remote-backend transport the remit requires to be protected.

evidence.snippet: "buildSshArgs: '-o', 'StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new' — first-seen host key auto-accepted for the remote-backend tunnel."

recommended_actions: Let operators pre-provision / pin the remote host key (or prompt for verification on first connect) instead of `accept-new`, so the tunnel carrying the API key is not exposed to first-connection interception.

findings.summary: The primary activity logs are free-form text rather than a structured schema, which narrows automated detection even though they are durable, rotated, redacted, and session-tagged.
findings.description: `agent.log` / `errors.log` / `gateway.log` are written with a plaintext format string (`%(asctime)s %(levelname)s%(session_tag)s %(name)s: %(message)s`), so the durable, operator-inspectable record the remit requires exists and is well-built (redaction, rotation, per-session tags, component routing) but is not machine-parseable at the action/decision level. Authoring reliable automated detection rules over free-form text is brittle; a structured (JSON-lines) action log would let the same events feed anomaly detection.
policy_rule_text: Agent actions and decisions are recorded to durable, operator-inspectable logs.

evidence.snippet: "_LOG_FORMAT = '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s%(session_tag)s %(name)s: %(message)s' — free-form text, no structured field schema."
"agent.log handler uses RedactingFormatter(_LOG_FORMAT); the main activity log is plaintext, not JSON lines."

recommended_actions: Add a structured (JSON-lines) action/decision log alongside the human-readable files so tool calls, approvals, and session events carry typed fields that automated detection can query.

findings.summary: Agent-writable persistent memory is a session-loaded persistence surface whose only pre-write screen is the same incomplete injection-pattern denylist.
findings.description: The agent writes to its own persistent memory, which is loaded back into later sessions' context — the canonical ASI06 persistence surface the remit's Risk Sensitivities call out. Writes are screened by `threat_patterns` at `strict` scope (a real, credited control that blocks classic injection, exfil, and persistence-backdoor patterns and invisible unicode), but that screen is a denylist over natural language and is incomplete by construction, so injected content that summarizes into memory can persist across sessions. Combined with the external-input-to-execution path (PRAX-2026-07-17-001), a poisoned memory entry is a cross-session foothold.

evidence.snippet: "strict-scope persistence/exfil patterns screen memory writes + skill installs; a denylist over prose, incomplete by construction (module notes shell/NL bypass surface)."
"first_threat_message() blocks a memory write on the first matched pattern — but only patterns in the list are caught."

recommended_actions: Add provenance tagging and an operator-reviewable audit trail for memory writes that originate from (or summarize) untrusted content, so a poisoned entry is detectable rather than silently loaded into every future session.

positives.title: Un-overridable provider-credential blocklist
positives.description: `_HERMES_PROVIDER_ENV_BLOCKLIST` strips Hermes-managed provider keys/tokens from shell, code-execution, and MCP child-process environments, and both the skill-frontmatter path and the config.yaml path refuse to register a blocklisted credential as passthrough — closing GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf so a malicious skill cannot re-export the credential into a sandbox.

positives.title: Unconditional hardline command floor beneath every bypass
positives.description: A tiny, deliberately-scoped blocklist (root-filesystem wipe, mkfs, dd-to-raw-device, fork bomb, kill -1, shutdown/reboot) is matched and blocked before the `--yolo` / `approvals.mode=off` / cron-approve bypass and has no override flag.

positives.title: Fail-closed external-surface authentication
positives.description: The API server refuses to start without `API_SERVER_KEY` (even on a loopback bind) and rejects placeholder keys on a network bind, while the gateway authorizes callers through a five-step check that ends in default-deny and treats session identifiers as routing handles, not authorization.

positives.title: Approval gate fails closed on timeout and terminal conflict
positives.description: An unanswered approval prompt resolves to `deny` after the configured window, and an approval requested while prompt_toolkit owns the terminal is denied fast rather than auto-approved — the human-in-the-loop gate never silently opens.

positives.title: Exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI
positives.description: Every direct dependency is pinned to an exact `==X.Y.Z` version with a committed `uv.lock` and CVE-annotated bumps, and CI enforces `osv-scanner`, `supply-chain-audit`, and `uv-lockfile-check`.

positives.title: Redacting, rotating, session-correlated logs
positives.description: All log files route through a `RedactingFormatter` so secrets are not written to disk, rotate under size caps, carry a per-session `[session_id]` tag injected via a global record factory, and separate gateway from agent records.

positives.title: Hardened Electron desktop process model
positives.description: Attached webviews are forced to `nodeIntegration=false`, `contextIsolation=true`, `sandbox=true`, `webSecurity=true`; navigation, redirect, and window-open are allowlisted to the app origin / loopback, and analytics disable session recording, respect Do-Not-Track, and mask PII.

rows.purpose: skill install/quarantine actions for operator review

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial, bordering Established. Hermes carries a coherent, layered, and consistently-applied trust architecture — fail-closed adapter and API authentication, an un-overridable credential blocklist and hardline command floor, exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI, and redacting rotating logs — that puts its strongest categories (Zero Trust, Supply Chain, Monitoring) at Established. It is held to Partial overall by the deliberate design choice that the default terminal backend is host-reaching while the agent ingests attacker-influenceable content, so the highest-value external-input-to-execution chain rests on in-process heuristics the project itself declines to treat as boundaries, and by the absence of a documented ongoing adversarial-testing program.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent is general-purpose by design (broad terminal/file/code/web/browser tool surface), but scope is really constrained — tools are operator-gated per platform via `hermes tools`, counterparty allowlists and DM-pairing bound who can drive it, and a documented swimlane names authorized domains; there is no hard topic gate, nor would one fit a personal assistant.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content (web, inbound messages, MCP responses, file reads) enters the model context by design, and the `threat_patterns` scanner plus tool-result delimiters screen it — but screening is an incomplete denylist and the agent's own writable persistent memory is a session-loaded persistence surface gated by that same heuristic, so untrusted-content controls are partial.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A consistently-applied set of operative controls — the API server refuses to start without `API_SERVER_KEY`, the gateway default-denies unknown callers, dangerous commands hit a human approval gate with an unconditional hardline floor beneath every bypass flag, and the credential blocklist scrubs child-process environments — establishes real interposition; the accepted, documented gap is that the default host-reaching backend leaves model-driven execution bounded by heuristics rather than OS isolation.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Every direct dependency is exact-pinned to `==X.Y.Z` with CVE-annotated bumps and a committed `uv.lock`, CI runs `osv-scanner`, `supply-chain-audit`, and `uv-lockfile-check`, and MCP package launches are checked against OSV malware advisories; the known gaps are the OSV check failing open on network error and third-party skill/plugin trust being delegated to operator review.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: There is concrete evidence of a security feedback loop — a fixed GHSA advisory hard-coded into the un-overridable credential blocklist (`GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf`), hardening ported from peer projects (openclaw#64586 placeholder-key guard, Mercury Agent's floor), and a large test suite — but no documented ongoing adversarial-testing program that drives architecture.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Durable, operator-inspectable logging is consistently applied — rotating `agent.log`/`errors.log`/`gateway.log` through a `RedactingFormatter`, per-session correlation tags, component routing, a skill-install audit log, and a session/state SQLite store; the known gap is that the primary activity logs are free-form text rather than a structured schema, which limits automated detection-rule authoring.
